questions.context: Globalization is the growing interdependence of the world's economies, cultures, and societies, produced by the flow of goods, capital, people, and information across national borders. [■] The term names not one event but a steady tightening of the links that bind distant communities together. A decision reached in one country, such as a sudden shift in consumer taste, can register within hours in markets on the far side of the planet. This tightening rests on the steadily falling cost of moving both things and ideas over long distances. Cheaper shipping, faster travel, and, above all, instant communication have shrunk the practical distance between nations. [■] Managers can therefore split the manufacture of a single product among many countries, placing each task wherever it can be done best or most cheaply. [■] The finished parts are then gathered together and shipped to consumers around the world. These dispersed networks reach well beyond the factory floor. Because national economies are now woven so tightly together, prosperity can spread more widely than before, yet so can risk. [■] However, a financial panic or a sudden shortage in one region can travel through the whole system within days, so the very links that spread wealth can also carry crises far and wide.

questions.context: Industrial organization refers to the way a firm arranges labor, machinery, and management to turn raw materials into finished goods. In the earliest workshops, a single skilled artisan built each product entirely, shaping and fitting every part by hand. [■] This method produced fine work but slowly, and because no two hand-made parts were alike, a broken item could rarely be repaired with pieces from another. Such craft production gave way, in the early factory era, to a division of labor in which each worker repeated one narrow task. Output rose sharply, yet the arrangement had a serious flaw. [■] Workers still carried materials from station to station, so unfinished goods piled up and idle time was common as faster hands waited for slower ones. [■] The pace of the whole line was set by its weakest point. This weakness was finally overcome by the moving assembly line, which reversed the old pattern by bringing the work to the worker. A powered belt carried each product past stationary workers, and its steady speed forced every task into a fixed rhythm. [■] Interchangeable parts, cut to standard sizes, snapped into place without adjustment. However, this rigid efficiency made the line hard to alter, so retooling for a new model could halt production for weeks.

questions.context: Plate tectonics is the theory that Earth's rigid outer shell is broken into several large plates that drift slowly over a hotter, softer layer below. Early in the twentieth century, the scientist Alfred Wegener proposed that the continents had once formed a single mass and had since moved apart. [■] Yet most geologists rejected the idea, because Wegener could name no force strong enough to push whole continents through the solid ocean floor. This missing mechanism was supplied decades later by studies of the deep ocean. Surveys revealed long undersea mountain ridges where molten rock rises, hardens, and spreads outward, widening the seabed on either side. [■] The rock on each flank carried matching magnetic stripes, mirror images proving that new floor forms symmetrically at the ridge. Continents, then, do not plow through the ocean floor; they ride slowly on top of it, like cargo on a conveyor belt. [■] This spreading seafloor tied together many scattered observations. Earthquakes, mountain belts, and the ranges of certain fossils all made sense once geologists mapped the plate boundaries where the moving slabs meet. [■] However, the theory still cannot predict exactly when the strain stored along these boundaries will break as an earthquake, so forecasting remains imprecise.

questions.context: Memory is the set of processes by which the brain encodes, stores, and later recovers information about past experience. Early researchers pictured it as an archive: an experience, once recorded, was thought to lie unchanged until needed, much as a book waits on a shelf. On this view, forgetting was merely a failure to find an intact record, not a change to it. This archival picture began to weaken as experiments revealed that remembering alters what is remembered. When people recalled an event, later questioning could reshape their account, folding in details that had only been suggested. The returning memory was not a clean copy but a version colored by everything learned since. Still, why the trace should change during recall remained unclear. This puzzle found its modern answer in a process called reconsolidation, in which a reactivated memory becomes briefly unstable and must be stored again. During this window the trace can be strengthened, weakened, or subtly rewritten before it settles. This casts memory less as a recording than as a living reconstruction, rebuilt slightly differently each use. However, because each retrieval can edit the trace, a vivid, confident memory is no guarantee of accuracy, a caution that courts weighing eyewitness testimony are only now absorbing.

questions.context: Organic compounds are substances built around frameworks of carbon atoms, and many of them occur in two forms that are mirror images, like a left and right hand. The two versions, called enantiomers, share identical bonds and formulas, yet they are not interchangeable. In living systems this difference is decisive: one form of a drug may heal, while its mirror image does nothing or even harms. A synthesis making both forms equally therefore wastes half its product and can carry real danger. This danger drove chemists to find ways to build just one mirror form while excluding the other. They developed special catalysts, themselves handed, that steer a reaction so incoming atoms can attach in only one orientation. Because a catalyst is not consumed, a small amount can direct the assembly of vast quantities of product, making the selective route practical on an industrial scale. This approach transformed the making of medicines, letting manufacturers produce the active form of a drug cleanly and discard little. It also lowered the risk of contaminating a remedy with a harmful twin. However, designing a catalyst precise enough for a given target remains slow and largely a matter of trial, so for many valuable compounds no efficient single-form route has yet been found.

questions.context: Organic compounds are substances built around frameworks of carbon atoms, and many of them occur in two forms that are mirror images, like a left and right hand. The two versions, called enantiomers, share identical bonds and formulas, yet they are not interchangeable. In living systems this difference is decisive: one form of a drug may heal, while its mirror image does nothing or even harms. A synthesis making both forms equally therefore wastes half its product and can carry real danger. This danger drove chemists to find ways to build just one mirror form while excluding the other. They developed special catalysts, themselves handed, that steer a reaction so incoming atoms can attach in only one orientation. Because a catalyst is not consumed, a small amount can direct the assembly of vast quantities of product, making the selective route practical on an industrial scale. This approach transformed the making of medicines, letting manufacturers produce the active form of a drug cleanly and discard little. It also lowered the risk of contaminating a remedy with a harmful twin. However, designing a catalyst precise enough for a given target remains slow and largely a matter of trial, so for many valuable compounds no efficient single-form route has yet been found.
questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the handed catalysts?

options.A: They must be replaced after each new batch of product.
options.B: A small quantity can produce a large amount of product.
options.C: They work only at very high temperatures.
options.D: They combine permanently with the finished product.

questions.subtopic: the role of oral traditions in preserving cultural knowledge
questions.context: Before writing existed, and in many communities long after it arrived, human knowledge survived through a single fragile channel: the human voice. Anthropologists study this as oral tradition, the practice of passing down history, law, genealogy, and technical know-how by spoken word from one generation to the next. It's easy to assume that a society without writing must have a shaky grip on its own past. But oral cultures developed remarkably reliable techniques for keeping their knowledge intact. Consider the griots of West Africa, professional oral historians who could recite the genealogies of ruling families going back centuries. A griot didn't simply remember facts. He wrapped them in rhythm, melody, and fixed poetic formulas, and those structures acted like scaffolding. Rhyme and meter, it turns out, are not mere decoration; they are error-correcting devices. When a line must scan a certain way, a mistaken word breaks the pattern, and the performer or the audience notices. Anthropologists have also found that oral knowledge is often distributed rather than centralized. Different specialists guard different bodies of lore, so no single failure erases everything. What emerges from all this is a reappraisal. For a long time scholars treated oral societies as primitive stages on the road to literacy. We now see something more interesting: sophisticated systems engineered, in their own way, for accuracy and endurance. Writing changed what humans could store, but it did not invent the human capacity to preserve knowledge across time. That capacity was already there, carried in performance, memory, and the disciplined art of the spoken word.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How oral traditions can reliably preserve knowledge without writing
options.B: The genealogies of West African ruling families and their griots
options.C: How the invention of writing transformed early human societies
options.D: Why traditional poetry relies on rhyme and meter for beauty

questions.subtopic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
questions.context: Think back to a vacation you took, or a long visit to the dentist. When you recall it later, you don't replay every single moment. Instead, your memory of the whole experience is dominated by just two points. Psychologists call this the peak-end rule. The idea, developed by Daniel Kahneman and his colleagues, is that we judge an experience largely by how it felt at its most intense moment, the peak, and by how it felt at the very end, rather than by averaging every moment along the way. In one striking study, patients undergoing an uncomfortable medical procedure were split into two groups. For one group, the procedure was left unchanged. For the other, doctors added a few extra minutes at the end during which the discomfort was mild rather than sharp. Objectively, that second group endured more total discomfort, since their procedure lasted longer. Yet afterward they remembered the whole experience as less unpleasant, and they were more willing to return. Why? Because the ending was gentler, and the ending carries disproportionate weight. What's remarkable here is the gap this reveals between two different selves: the self that lives through an experience moment by moment, and the self that remembers it afterward. These two don't always agree. The remembering self, which drives our future decisions, cares intensely about peaks and endings and is surprisingly indifferent to how long something lasted. That's why a brief but terrible finish can spoil an otherwise pleasant day, and why a rough experience that ends well can be recalled almost fondly.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How doctors can make medical procedures shorter and less painful
options.B: A rule describing how peaks and endings shape our memories
options.C: The results of a single study on patients' medical discomfort
options.D: Why long vacations often feel much shorter than they really were

The idea, developed by Daniel Kahneman and his colleagues, is that we judge an experience largely by how it felt at its most intense moment, the peak, and by how it felt at the very end, rather than by averaging every moment along the way. In one striking study, patients undergoing an uncomfortable medical procedure were split into two groups. For one group, the procedure was left unchanged. For the other, doctors added a few extra minutes at the end during which the discomfort was mild rather than sharp. Objectively, that second group endured more total discomfort, since their procedure lasted longer. Yet afterward they remembered the whole experience as less unpleasant, and they were more willing to return. Why? Because the ending was gentler, and the ending carries disproportionate weight. What's remarkable here is the gap this reveals between two different selves: the self that lives through an experience moment by moment, and the self that remembers it afterward. These two don't always agree. The remembering self, which drives our future decisions, cares intensely about peaks and endings and is surprisingly indifferent to how long something lasted. That's why a brief but terrible finish can spoil an otherwise pleasant day, and why a rough experience that ends well can be recalled almost fondly.
questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the study find about the group whose procedure was extended?

options.A: They felt sharper, more intense pain throughout the whole procedure
options.B: They later asked their doctors to shorten all future procedures
options.C: They remembered the experience as less unpleasant overall
options.D: They endured less total discomfort than the unchanged group

questions.subtopic: the role of wetlands in flood prevention
questions.context: When a river swells after heavy rain, we often picture concrete dams and levees holding the water back. But some of the most effective flood defenses aren't built by engineers at all. They're wetlands, the marshes, swamps, and bogs that sit along rivers and coastlines. A wetland is land where the soil is saturated with water either permanently or seasonally, and in flood terms it behaves like a giant sponge. Here's how it works. During a heavy storm, a wetland soaks up enormous volumes of water into its spongy soils and dense vegetation. Instead of rushing downstream all at once, the water is held and then released slowly over days or weeks. This flattens the flood peak, the dangerous surge that would otherwise overwhelm towns downstream. Consider coastal marshes during storms. Their thick grasses and muddy ground absorb the force of incoming surges, so less water and energy reach the land behind them. Studies after major hurricanes have estimated that coastal wetlands prevent billions of dollars in flood damage every year. And unlike a concrete barrier, a wetland maintains itself and can even improve with time. The lesson for planners is significant. For decades, wetlands were drained and filled to make room for farms and housing, and only later did communities discover that they had removed a natural buffer. Today many regions are restoring wetlands precisely for their flood protection, alongside the wildlife habitat and water filtering they also provide. Sometimes the smartest engineering is simply letting a natural system do what it has always done.

options.A: How engineers design concrete dams and levees for rivers
options.B: The damage that major hurricanes cause to coastal towns
options.C: How wetlands protect nearby areas from flooding naturally
options.D: How wetlands are drained to create new farmland and housing

questions.subtopic: the rediscovery of perspective in early Renaissance painting
questions.context: Stand in front of a medieval painting and something may strike you as slightly off. Figures seem to float, distant objects are the same size as near ones, and the space feels flat, almost like a stage backdrop. Then, in early fifteenth-century Italy, something changed. Painters rediscovered a technique called linear perspective, a mathematical system for representing three-dimensional space on a flat surface. The core idea is that parallel lines appear to converge as they recede, meeting at a single vanishing point on the horizon. The architect Filippo Brunelleschi is usually credited with working out the geometry around 1420, and his friend Leon Battista Alberti soon wrote it down as a set of rules any painter could follow. Suddenly a painted room could seem to open up behind the picture's surface, inviting the viewer's eye to travel into a convincing depth. I say rediscovered deliberately. The ancient Greeks and Romans had grasped aspects of perspective, but that knowledge faded during the medieval centuries. What the Renaissance added was rigor: perspective became a precise, teachable geometry rather than an intuitive trick. And notice what this reflects. Perspective wasn't just a painting technique; it's an expression of a new confidence that the visible world could be measured and mastered through reason, the very spirit driving Renaissance science and mathematics. A painting organized around a vanishing point places the individual viewer at a single, privileged point of view. In that sense, the humble vanishing point mirrors a much larger shift in how Europeans were beginning to see themselves and their place in the world.

options.A: How the ancient Greeks and Romans painted realistic scenes
options.B: Why figures in medieval paintings appear to float in space
options.C: How Brunelleschi designed buildings in fifteenth-century Italy
options.D: The rediscovery of linear perspective and what it reflected
